blocks.text: If z is a function of x and y, i.e., z = f(x, y), the total differential dz is obtained from the partial differentials dx and dy by the use of the following relation:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: The total differential of z in terms of its partial derivatives with respect to x and y.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of x and y, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like z, x, and y.

blocks.description: The figure illustrates the relationship between points on a surface and their projections onto the xy-plane, showing the change in position Q near point P in terms of rising or falling on the surface as influenced by changes in x and y. It highlights how the change in z relates to the changes in its independent variables through the tangent plane at point P.
This equation expresses the total rate of change of z concerning a parameter t as a function of the partial rates of change with respect to x and y.

blocks.text: Let P be a point (x, y, z) on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P on the surface. The change of position is most easily related to the diagram (Fig. 21).
